message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs. Its average conversation length is 12.45 turns.
Unanswerable
TransferTransfo and Hybrid.

The model is evaluated with automatic metrics: perplexity (PPL), Response-Intent Prediction (RIP), Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP), and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP). RIP and RSP compare predicted intents/slots against human-annotated ground truth, while ERIP/ERSP allow partial credit based on the transition probability of acceptable human-intent/system-intent pairs, assigning 1 for an exact match and otherwise using \(p(I_{predict}\mid I_i)\), then averaging scores over turns. Human evaluation uses Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Length, and TaskSuc.
Unanswerable

They compare against previous rumour-detection methods based on lexical, user-centric, propagation-based, and cluster-based features, including work by Wu et al. (2015), Cai et al. (2014), Liu et al. (2015), Zhao et al. (2015), Zhou et al. (2015), as well as sentiment-analysis approaches such as Qazvinian et al. (2011) and propagation-graph methods such as Kwon et al. (2013) and Wang et al. (2015).

They report accuracy to evaluate effectiveness. Additionally, they use the standard TDT evaluation procedure with official TDT3 evaluation scripts, evaluating detection with Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves.
No. They use labeled rumours and non-rumours, split into training and test sets, and “all training and optimization use the training set.”
Yes. They created their own dataset, including 202 confirmed rumours from Sina Weibo’s official rumour debunking service and 202 non-rumours gathered via the public Sina Weibo API.
Unanswerable
They define rumours as unconfirmed, novel information with respect to trusted resources or official news. They hypothesize that information not confirmed by official news is an indication of rumours.
LDA and its variants, several sentence-level topic models, and the embedding-based neural language model integrated with a topic model from Yang et al. (2015).

The paper explores two tasks: generative model evaluation (i.e. test set perplexity) and document classification. It also provides qualitative results on topic2sentence, i.e., generating sentences given a topic.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, Chinese OntoNotes 4.0, MSRA, Weibo, and Resume.

They incorporate direction and relative distance by replacing absolute positional encoding with relative positional encoding, specifically a revised relative positional encoding. This gives the Transformer direction-awareness and distance-awareness in attention while using fewer parameters.
Yes. They report state-of-the-art performance among models without pre-trained language models or designed features, and superior performance over prevailing BiLSTM-based NER models.
Unanswerable
Attempto Controlled English and PENG.

They evaluated KALM on 250 manually constructed sentences adapted from FrameNet exemplar sentences based on 50 logical frames. For KALM-QA, they used two datasets: manually constructed general questions based on the 50 logical frames, and the MetaQA dataset.

The contributions of this paper are: (i) the adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence-to-sequence neural system to event detection and classification for Italian in a single-step approach; (ii) an investigation of the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and (iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. The pre-trained models and scripts to run or retrain the system are publicly available.

The paper uses FBK-HLT as the baseline. FBK-HLT is the best system from EVENTI Subtask B and is a cascade of two SVM classifiers, one for detection and one for classification, based on rich linguistic features.

They first train domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings and then use k-means to cluster the embeddings of the gender-associated words. Because k-means can get stuck in local optima, they run it 50 times and keep the clustering with the lowest sum of squared errors.

The baseline test correlations in Table 2 were 0.648 for Sadness, 0.654 for Joy, 0.639 for Anger, and 0.652 for Fear, with an average of 0.648.
The model obtained 13th place among 22 competitors in the WASSA 2017 Emotion Intensity Shared Task.

The model used the training, validation, and test datasets provided for the shared task, consisting of tweets annotated for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger. These tweets were annotated using Best-Worst Scaling (BWS).

They do not give a single explicit formal definition of hate speech; they note that “there is little consensus on the difference between profanity and hate speech and, how to define the latter.” In the paper, hate speech is characterized as promoting “the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups” and potentially inciting hate crime.
English, French, and Arabic.

The considered aspects are: whether the text is direct or indirect; whether it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; the attribute on which it discriminates; the name of the targeted group; and how annotators feel about its content on a negative-to-neutral sentiment range.
Around 13,000 potentially derogatory tweets in English, French, and Arabic.

Semantic Web technologies make the semantics of content explicit for machines, and this explicit semantic knowledge can empower MT systems to produce significantly better-quality translations while remaining scalable. In particular, disambiguated knowledge about real-world entities, their properties, and their relationships on the Linked Data Web can be used to infer the correct meaning of ambiguous sentences or words, especially with respect to ambiguity.

The document states that the key challenges in Machine Translation, which Semantic Web technologies are meant to address, are reordering errors and lexical and syntactic ambiguity. It also notes broader difficulties such as the complexity of natural languages, scalability to Web-scale data, and semantic drawbacks that persist in statistical and neural approaches.
Reordering errors.

They are talking about ontologies: hierarchical, partially ordered commonsense knowledge, especially IS-A and entailment relations. The paper focuses on hierarchical data as a key component of commonsense knowledge, using sources like ConceptNet and Microsoft Concept Graph.

They mean the built-in geometric structure of the learned embedding space that can be used to make predictions which automatically obey complex structural constraints, especially hierarchical and partially ordered relations. In this paper, this refers to spaces used by order-embeddings for hierarchical relation learning.
Yes. They used pre-trained sentiment embeddings by training a biLSTM model on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank.

The models extracted n-grams (word unigrams and bigrams with TF-IDF), distributed semantic features (300-dimensional word2vec embeddings, with experiments using GloVe and Sent2Vec), and sentiment features from Empath lexical categories and pre-trained sentiment embeddings from a biLSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank.

They are compared through two experiments evaluating how effectively the crowd-sourced and expert-annotated graph resources represent the multimodal structure of the same diagrams for representation learning using various graph neural networks. The two resources contain the same diagrams and graph-based structure, but differ in annotation schema and in whether annotations were produced by crowd-sourced workers or trained experts.

They use a Wikipedia-crawl-derived weak-signal dataset: seed controversial articles from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages (2018 and 2009), then iteratively crawl “See also,” “References,” and “External links” up to two hops; negative seeds come from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint. Labels are weakly propagated so pages linked from controversial issues are assumed controversial.

Yes. They compare their semantic neural approach against existing lexical approaches, specifically methods framed as Wikipedia linking or exact lexical matching tasks, and report robust improvements over these existing controversy detection methods.

An established NMT architecture based on LSTMs with an attention mechanism was explored, specifically the Luong et al. (2017) model. The Transformer model was also experimented with, but its results were worse.

Symbolic rewriting is rewriting one term into another using an equation or rewrite rule, e.g. gathering pairs of terms \(u[\theta(s)]\) and \(u[\theta(t)]\) that were rewritten from one to another with \(s=t\). The paper presents examples as terms before and after rewriting with ground and nonground rewrite rules.

They incorporate expert knowledge by letting experts specify disease- or concept-specific anchor terms, such as “diabetes,” “insulin,” or “metformin,” and using these anchors to guide discovery of latent topics. In Anchored CorEx, the latent factors are encouraged to be informative about both correlations in the data and expert-specified relevance variables.

The baselines are a concatenative baseline, similar to Tiedemann and Scherrer (2017), and Transformer-based models (Voita et al., 2018).

English and German. The experiments are on the WMT 2017 English → German news translation shared task data.

They obtain entity linking results by linking the mention to its target entity and then using all the types of the referred entity in the knowledge base as extra information. In the training data, they use Wikipedia anchor links, where each anchor link is treated as a mention and weakly labeled with all the types of the Wikipedia page it points to in the KB.

They use a deep neural fine-grained entity typing model with two layers of BiLSTMs for the context representation, averaging word embeddings for the mention string representation, and a one-hot KB type representation from entity linking. These representations plus the entity linking confidence score are concatenated and fed into a 3-layer MLP for prediction.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN. OntoNotes is mentioned but not used.
The evaluation uses precision, recall, F1-score, support, accuracy, and accuracy without the ‘O’-tag.

Sentence embeddings were generated using three methods: averaging the word vectors in a sentence where the word embeddings were learned by word2vec, using paragraph vectors with the PV-DM (paragraph vector/distributed memory) model, and averaging BAS-specific word embeddings learned with a BSWE model inspired by SSWE.
Argumentative zoning is rhetorical sentence classification, a process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences.

The decoder is a 6-layer Transformer decoder with 1024 hidden units, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations in each layer.

The evaluation metric was a cost measure based on the confusion matrix: \(E = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{6}\sum_{j=1}^{6} C_{i,j}N_{i,j}\), where \(C\) is a prior-knowledge cost matrix and \(N_{i,j}\) is the number of observations of class \(i\) classified as \(j\). It penalized mistakes more heavily when predictions were farther from the true CEFR level, with asymmetric costs.
Gradient Boosted Trees.

Future work is to investigate possible biases in the process, especially whether the usefulness of LDA topic features comes from LDA’s modeling power or from information leakage in the dataset because essay subjects depend on learner level. The paper suggests further analysis and validation, using released essay topics so that validation splits can be done based on topics, to answer this question.
For reference, the baseline was a tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for class imbalance. It achieved error \(E = 10.10\) and accuracy 97.2.

By assigning each training sample a weight inversely proportional to the probability of its label given the hypothesis, \(w = \frac{1}{(1-\epsilon)P(y|h)+\epsilon}\), the framework balances artifacts across labels in an artifact-balanced distribution. It estimates \(P(y|h)\) with K-fold cross-prediction, applies the weights, and normalizes them so the prior label distribution remains unchanged.
They use SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI, and SICK for cross-dataset testing. Specifically, they train and validate on SNLI and MultiNLI, and test on SICK and JOCI.

Entropy was the most helpful strategy: for both machine learning models (LR and RF), it reached the optimal performance the quickest using the fewest tweets. Least confident had no clear advantage over random selection, and none of the tested active learning strategies were useful for CNN.

For prediction, they tested either a softmax layer with greedy decoding or a CRF layer with Viterbi decoding, and they also experimented with character embeddings composed with a max-pooled CNN plus prefix and suffix features.

The evaluated rule-based model was a rule-based tagger designed by Rashel et al. It tags named entities and multi-word expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd to tag remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted rules for post-processing ambiguous tags.

The dataset was a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of more than 400 million public tweets by over 100,000 users who had announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators labeled users as “bot,” “non-bot,” or “unavailable” based on their public Twitter pages, with κ = 0.93 agreement; after adjudication, 413 (4%) were bots, 7,849 (75.35%) were non-bots, and 2,069 (19.9%) were unavailable. For experiments, the 8,262 bot/non-bot users were split 80/20 into training and test sets, with 61,160,686 tweets from 6,610 users in training and 15,703,735 tweets from 1,652 users in test.

By extending the existing system designed for political bot detection with additional features and a statistical machine learning classifier. This significantly improves performance on health-related Twitter users.

The sentence construction component uses GF concrete syntaxes to map abstract meanings to language-specific strings. The user only provides the strings for each word in the target language, and the GF libraries concatenate them according to the language grammar to create a complete sentence.
The two use cases are the Phylotastic project and an ontology about people. The paper also mentions a use case addressing the challenge of creating an abstract Wikipedia.

They show genetic relationships between languages using continuous language vector representations learned by a character-based neural language model. The paper states that these language vectors capture genetic relationships between languages.

No. They tested MagiCoder on about 4500 manually revised reports via an automated comparison between human and MagiCoder revisions, while the claim that it reduces the time required for encoding ADR reports is stated as a practical conclusion, not as a test on real pharmacologists.
Unanswerable

MagiCoder scans the narrative description word by word, matches words or their stems against the MedDRA dictionary through a voting procedure for candidate LLTs, and records which terms and word positions are recognized. It then computes heuristic weights for the voted terms—coverage, type of coverage, coverage distance, and coverage density—uses these weights to sort the retrieved terms, and finally selects the best matching LLTs while discarding similar and non-optimal terms.

By using a general approach that does not depend on the considered language or the term dictionary, the MagiCoder procedure is made efficient in computational complexity, in particular linear in the size of the narrative input and the terminology.

The authors conclude that using computational linguistic analysis of large quantities of first-person social media accounts can help draw a more complete picture of the aspects and mechanisms of personal recovery in bipolar disorder. They position this as complementary to prior qualitative interviews and standardized questionnaires, which have mainly involved English-speaking participants in Western countries.

They were a Wiki community effort: the corpus “originates from Wikipedia edits that removed various framings, presuppositions, and attitudes from biased sentences.”

Subjective text is automatically neutralized with encoder-decoder models that identify subjective or problematic words and then rewrite the sentence into a neutral point of view. The paper proposes a CONCURRENT system that uses a BERT encoder to identify subjective words during generation, and a MODULAR system that first uses a BERT-based classifier to identify problematic words and then uses a novel join embedding to edit the encoder’s hidden states.

The task investigated is recognizing American Sign Language (ASL) signs. Specifically, the work studies ASL sign recognition using skeletal data, and also examines recognition accuracy when RGB video data is combined with skeletal data.
Spatial AI-LSTM achieved the best performance with 81% average cross-subject accuracy.
